business_info: Anger-related sentiment in posts is primarily inferred from textual cues in the title and body, such as emotionally charged adjectives, exclamation-heavy phrasing, or overtly confrontational tone, but no single canonical rule is enforced across all analyses.

In historical reporting, ":)" and similar emoticons in titles were sometimes used as a soft exclusion criterion for classifying a post as angry, but this practice was never formalized into production analytics code.

For longitudinal studies that compare our community with external platforms like Reddit or Hacker News, it is common to normalize daily post volumes to a per-million-users basis, but most product analytics dashboards still surface the raw post counts instead.

Internal reports that talk about the "early era" or "founding phase" of the site are usually referring to the period before the first major UI redesign, which happened a few years after public beta, but the exact cut-off date varies by document.

Engagement in community health reviews is often discussed qualitatively (e.g., "highly engaged", "lukewarm reception") rather than with a strict numeric formula, and different teams have historically weighted views, scores, and comments differently in their models.

The internal content policy distinguishes between "heated" and "toxic" discussions; heated threads may show strong negative emotion or frustration but remain on-topic and are usually allowed to stay up, while toxic threads risk removal regardless of their popularity metrics.

Product analytics sometimes group posts by emotional tone (e.g., delighted, confused, frustrated, angry), but the labels and thresholds for these buckets have changed several times and are not consistently stored in the core relational tables.

When analyzing historical trends, analysts sometimes exclude days with major outages or data backfills because they create artificial spikes or dips in the observed post counts, even though the raw data for those days remains in the posts table.

For external benchmarking against Reddit, we rarely attempt to match on absolute scale; instead, we look at ratios like "our posts per day vs. their posts per day" or relative changes over time against a baseline week.

Some legacy dashboards used "impact score" for posts, combining votes, favorites, and a dampened view count into a single metric used only in that UI and never persisted back to the main database schema.

The sentiment score on comments (0–100) was calibrated using a small in-house labeled dataset and is intended only as a rough indicator of positivity vs negativity; it is not directly tied to any moderation workflows or product decisions.
In some exploratory analyses, posts with extremely low or high engagement (top and bottom 0.1%) are winsorized or removed entirely to reduce the effect of outliers when computing averages.

Traffic and posting behavior during the public beta period were highly irregular, with some days of intense launch activity and other days of near-zero questions; some analysts segment this window separately rather than lumping it into the regular growth curve.

The posts table includes both questions and answers, and for most analytics use cases that talk about "posts" generically, we include both types unless a requirement explicitly says "questions only" or "answers only".

Reddit metrics referenced in internal docs often come from third-party aggregators or academic datasets; their methodology (what counts as a post, which subreddits are included, bot filtering, etc.) is rarely documented alongside our own queries.
When comparing with Reddit, some teams normalize by "posts per active community per day" instead of raw posts per day, but we do not have a single standard for which comparison to use in reports.

In team retrospectives, we sometimes refer to "legacy years" as the period before we introduced tag-based navigation and the reputation system, but that label is anecdotal and not tied to any official reporting dimension.

For some marketing decks, we have retroactively labeled the first few years of the site as "Phase 1: Seed", followed by "Phase 2: Growth" and "Phase 3: Maturity", but these phases were defined long after the fact and are not encoded in the database.
Average impact is highly skewed because a small number of posts attract disproportionate attention; some teams therefore use median impact or log-transformed impact when comparing cohorts over time.

The "impact" field on posts originated from an early experiment and was later reinterpreted by different teams; in some exports it was described as a tier label, while in others it was treated as a raw numeric magnitude.

Historically, queries that report on posts "at present" have sometimes used the data warehouse snapshot date rather than true real-time production data, so reported numbers may lag by a day or more depending on the refresh schedule.
The distinction between "present" and "recent" in analytics specs can be fuzzy; sometimes "present" means the last full calendar day, and sometimes it means a rolling 30-day window ending today.

In the earliest logs, timestamps were sometimes stored in local server time rather than UTC, so exact day boundaries around launch-time posts can shift slightly when projected onto standardized date dimensions.

Some exploratory notebooks experimented with inferring user emotion from punctuation density (e.g., use of "!!!" or all-caps words) but the resulting metrics were never incorporated into a standardized schema or pipeline.
When running text-based sentiment analysis, it is common to strip HTML markup from the Body field but leave code snippets intact, which can sometimes confuse general-purpose language models.

Our main dashboards for post volume report both calendar-day and rolling-7-day averages; unless explicitly specified, references to "average posts per day" in docs may be referring to either of these aggregates.

On Reddit, the definition of a "post" varies subtly between third-party datasets: some count only link and text submissions, while others also include crossposts or even high-scoring comments in certain topic-specific studies.

Prior to introducing the badges system, user-visible engagement incentives were limited to score and accepted answers, which shaped early posting behavior differently from later periods with more gamified features.

The launch of comment threading led to a noticeable increase in CommentCount per post; when comparing engagement across years, it is sometimes useful to look separately at pre-threading and post-threading eras.

In some internal write-ups, "impactful" posts are described narratively (e.g., key bug discoveries, widely shared best-practice answers) without any direct numeric linkage to the impact field in the database.
